title: Pelea grandifolia
intro: Pelea grandifolia, also known as the Tahina or Tahina palm, is a species of flowering plant in the family Rutaceae, native to the island of Madagascar. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities, which may help to alleviate symptoms of various conditions such as arthritis, wounds, and respiratory disorders. The bioactive constituents of Pelea grandifolia include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include teas, infusions, and extracts, which can be used to harness its medicinal benefits.
taxonomy: Pelea grandifolia is a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae. The genus Pelea is a part of the subfamily Rauvolfioideae. It is native to Hawaii and is a rare and endangered species. The classification of Pelea grandifolia places it within the kingdom Plantae and the phylum Angiospermophyta. Further classification includes the class Magnoliopsida and the order Gentianales.
uses: Pelea grandifolia is a plant species native to the tropical forests of Hawaii, and its medicinal uses are still being studied. constituents: Pelea grandifolia, a species of flowering plant, is known to contain various active constituents, including flavonoids, which have been reported to exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.
